title: All-or-Nothing Thinking
summary: Viewing situations in extreme black-and-white terms.
detailed_explanation: This cognitive distortion leads to seeing things as complete success or total failure.
when_to_use: When you catch yourself using words like 'always' or 'never'.
when_not_to_use: During acute crisis.
step_by_step_exercise: Identify the thought
Find middle ground
Reframe
reflection_questions: What would a friend say?
common_mistakes: Ignoring nuance
example_scenario: One mistake = total failure.

title: Box Breathing (Four-Square Breathing)
summary: A structured breathing pattern that paces inhale, hold, exhale, and hold for equal counts to calm the nervous system.
detailed_explanation: Box breathing slows respiration rate and lengthens exhalation, which stimulates the vagus nerve and activates the parasympathetic ('rest and digest') nervous system, counteracting the fight-or-flight stress response. The equal-count structure also gives the mind something concrete to focus on, interrupting anxious or racing thoughts. It's used by clinicians, athletes, and even military personnel to regulate arousal quickly.
when_to_use: When feeling anxious, overwhelmed, panicky, or before a stressful event (e.g., a difficult conversation, exam, or public speaking); useful as a quick reset during the day.
when_not_to_use: During a panic attack with severe shortness of breath or asthma symptoms where breath-holding could increase distress; if dizziness or lightheadedness occurs, stop and switch to natural breathing; not a substitute for medical care during respiratory distress.
step_by_step_exercise: Sit or stand comfortably with a straight but relaxed spine.
Exhale all the air from your lungs slowly through your mouth.
Inhale quietly through your nose while counting to 4.
Hold your breath for a count of 4.
Exhale slowly through your mouth for a count of 4.
Hold the empty breath for a count of 4.
Repeat the cycle for 4-8 rounds, adjusting the count (e.g., 3 or 5) to whatever feels comfortable.
reflection_questions: What did you notice in your body before versus after the exercise?
Did any particular part of the cycle (the holds, the exhale) feel harder or easier?
common_mistakes: Forcing the breath or holding too long until it feels strained
Breathing shallowly from the chest instead of the belly
Giving up after one round instead of allowing several cycles for the effect to build
example_scenario: User: 'My heart is racing before this presentation.' Companion: 'Let's try box breathing together, it only takes about a minute. Breathe in for 4 counts... hold for 4... out for 4... hold for 4. I'll count with you.'

title: 5-4-3-2-1 Sensory Grounding
summary: A sensory-based grounding exercise that uses the five senses to anchor attention in the present moment.
detailed_explanation: This technique interrupts spirals of anxiety, dissociation, or rumination by deliberately redirecting attention to concrete sensory input, which is incompatible with getting lost in anxious future-thinking or painful memories. It works because the brain can't fully attend to abstract worry and detailed present-moment sensory scanning at the same time, making it a fast way to shift out of a threat-focused state.
when_to_use: During moments of high anxiety, panic, overwhelm, dissociation, or when racing thoughts feel impossible to stop; useful in public or busy settings since it can be done silently.
when_not_to_use: During severe dissociation where the person cannot orient to their surroundings at all (simpler orienting like stating name, date, and location may be needed first, ideally with professional support); not a substitute for crisis intervention if there is risk of self-harm.
step_by_step_exercise: Pause and take one slow breath.
Name 5 things you can see around you right now.
Name 4 things you can physically feel (e.g., feet on the floor, fabric on skin).
Name 3 things you can hear.
Name 2 things you can smell (or two scents you like).
Name 1 thing you can taste (or one thing you appreciate about yourself).
reflection_questions: Which sense was easiest to notice? Which was hardest?
How does your body feel now compared to when you started?
common_mistakes: Rushing through the list without actually pausing to notice each item
Doing it purely mentally when saying it out loud or writing it down often works better
Expecting complete calm instead of a gradual, partial shift
example_scenario: User: 'I feel like I'm spiraling and can't focus on anything.' Companion: 'Let's ground together. Can you name 5 things you can see right now? Just look around and list them for me.'

title: Hand on Heart (Supportive Touch)
summary: Placing a hand on your heart or another comforting spot to physically cue feelings of safety and self-kindness.
detailed_explanation: Supportive touch activates the mammalian caregiving system, releasing oxytocin and lowering cortisol, similar to the comfort of a hug from another person. Kristin Neff's research found that even self-administered touch can lower physiological stress markers and increase feelings of being cared for. It gives an embodied, non-verbal way to signal safety to the nervous system when words alone feel hollow.
when_to_use: In moments of sadness, shame, self-criticism, or stress, especially when you need comfort but no one else is available; can be paired with other self-compassion practices.
when_not_to_use: If touch feels distressing or triggering due to trauma history, in which case another self-soothing gesture (e.g., wrapping arms around oneself, holding a warm mug) can be offered instead; not a replacement for connection with others when isolation itself is the problem.
step_by_step_exercise: Notice you are struggling or in pain.
Place one or both hands gently over your heart, or wherever feels soothing (cheek, arms, stomach).
Feel the warmth and gentle pressure of your hand against your body.
Take a few slow breaths, allowing your shoulders to soften.
Silently offer yourself a kind phrase, such as 'I'm here with you' or 'This is hard, and that's okay.'
reflection_questions: What did you notice in your body as you did this?
Was it easy or hard to let yourself receive this small act of comfort?
common_mistakes: Doing the gesture mechanically without pausing to actually feel it
Feeling self-conscious and skipping it because it 'feels silly', when the benefit comes from doing it anyway
Expecting it to erase the difficult emotion rather than simply offering comfort alongside it
example_scenario: User: 'I just feel so alone right now.' Companion: 'That sounds really painful. Would you be willing to try something small, placing a hand on your heart, and just noticing the warmth there for a few breaths, like you're letting yourself know you're not facing this completely alone?'

title: Self-Compassion Break
summary: A brief three-step practice, developed by Dr. Kristin Neff, that brings mindfulness, common humanity, and self-kindness to a moment of suffering.
detailed_explanation: This practice directly targets the three components Neff identified as core to self-compassion: acknowledging pain without exaggerating or suppressing it (mindfulness), remembering that struggle is part of the shared human experience rather than a personal failing (common humanity), and actively offering oneself warmth instead of judgment (self-kindness). Research links this combination to lower anxiety, depression, and rumination compared to self-criticism.
when_to_use: In any moment of emotional pain, disappointment, failure, or self-criticism, especially when your inner voice has turned harsh.
when_not_to_use: During acute crisis where more direct stabilization or professional support is needed; if self-compassion language feels aversive at first for some trauma survivors, a gentler, more gradual introduction with a therapist may be more appropriate.
step_by_step_exercise: Bring to mind the difficulty you are facing and let yourself feel it in your body.
Say to yourself, silently or aloud, 'This is a moment of suffering' (Mindfulness).
Say, 'Suffering is part of life, other people feel this way too' (Common Humanity).
Say, 'May I be kind to myself' or 'May I give myself the compassion I need' (Self-Kindness).
Optionally, pair this with a soothing gesture like a hand on your heart.
reflection_questions: Which of the three steps felt most natural, and which felt hardest to believe?
What would you say to a friend going through this same situation?
common_mistakes: Skipping the 'common humanity' step and going straight to self-kindness, which can leave the pain feeling isolating
Using the phrases as a way to bypass or dismiss the pain rather than acknowledging it first
Expecting instant relief rather than a shift in how you relate to the pain
example_scenario: User: 'I completely messed up that interview, I'm so stupid.' Companion: 'That sounds really discouraging. Can we pause for a second? First, just notice, this is a moment of real disappointment. Second, almost everyone has bombed an interview at some point, you're not alone in this. And third, what if you tried offering yourself a bit of kindness right now, the way you would to a friend in this situation?'

title: Progressive Muscle Relaxation (Short Version)
summary: A technique of deliberately tensing and then releasing muscle groups to reduce physical tension and promote relaxation.
detailed_explanation: Developed by Edmund Jacobson, PMR is based on the principle that mental relaxation naturally follows physical relaxation, and that people often hold unnoticed chronic tension without realizing it. Deliberately tensing a muscle first makes the subsequent release more noticeable and complete, teaching the body to recognize and let go of tension patterns associated with stress and anxiety.
when_to_use: When experiencing physical tension, restlessness, difficulty falling asleep, or generalized anxiety; useful as a wind-down routine before bed.
when_not_to_use: If there are muscle injuries, chronic pain conditions, or cardiovascular issues where tensing muscles could cause harm or discomfort (consult a doctor first); skip or modify if tensing certain areas causes pain.
step_by_step_exercise: Get into a comfortable seated or lying position.
Starting with your hands, clench your fists tightly for 5 seconds, then release and notice the relaxation for 10 seconds.
Move to your arms: tense your biceps for 5 seconds, then release.
Tense your shoulders up toward your ears for 5 seconds, then release.
Scrunch your face muscles for 5 seconds, then release.
Tense your stomach for 5 seconds, then release.
Tense your legs and feet for 5 seconds, then release.
Take a final moment to notice the overall sense of looseness across your body.
reflection_questions: Which muscle group was holding the most tension without you realizing it?
How does your body feel now compared to before you started?
common_mistakes: Tensing muscles too hard to the point of pain or cramping
Rushing through without pausing to actually feel the contrast between tension and release
Holding your breath while tensing instead of breathing normally
example_scenario: User: 'I can't fall asleep, my body just feels wound up.' Companion: 'Let's try a quick progressive muscle relaxation before bed. Start by clenching your fists tight for 5 seconds... now let go and notice the difference. We'll work up through your body like that.'

title: Emotional Labeling ("Name It to Tame It")
summary: Putting a specific word to what you're feeling to reduce the emotion's intensity and regain a sense of control.
detailed_explanation: Neuroscience research, notably by Matthew Lieberman, shows that verbally labeling an emotion activates the prefrontal cortex and dampens activity in the amygdala, the brain's threat-detection center. This means simply naming a feeling, such as 'I'm anxious' rather than just being consumed by anxiety, can measurably reduce its intensity, because it shifts processing from a purely reactive, emotional mode into a more reflective, regulated one.
when_to_use: When feeling emotionally flooded, reactive, or unable to identify what's going on internally; useful as a first step before any other coping strategy.
when_not_to_use: If naming the emotion leads to over-analyzing or spiraling into rumination rather than simply noticing and moving forward; during acute crisis, prioritize safety and stabilization over emotional analysis.
step_by_step_exercise: Pause and turn your attention inward.
Ask yourself, 'What am I feeling right now?'
Try to name the emotion with a specific word (e.g., 'frustrated' instead of just 'bad'); a feelings wheel can help if you're stuck.
Notice where in your body you feel this emotion.
Say to yourself, 'I'm noticing I feel ___,' rather than 'I am ___' (e.g., 'I'm noticing I feel anxious' rather than 'I am anxious').
Let the label sit without needing to immediately fix or change the feeling.
reflection_questions: Was there more than one emotion present at once?
Did naming the feeling change its intensity at all?
common_mistakes: Reaching for vague labels like 'bad' or 'fine' instead of more specific words
Fusing with the emotion ('I am my anxiety') rather than observing it ('I notice anxiety here')
Using labeling as a way to judge the emotion as good or bad instead of simply noticing it
example_scenario: User: 'I don't even know what I'm feeling, just this awful mess.' Companion: 'That sounds overwhelming. Let's slow down for a second, if you had to pick one or two words for what's underneath the mess, what would they be? Sad? Angry? Scared?'

title: Values Reflection
summary: An exercise to identify what truly matters to you so your actions can be guided by your values rather than by fear or avoidance.
detailed_explanation: In Acceptance and Commitment Therapy, values are understood as chosen life directions, like 'being a caring friend' or 'growing intellectually', rather than goals to complete. Clarifying values gives a compass for decision-making and helps people act meaningfully even alongside difficult emotions, rather than organizing life around avoiding discomfort, which research links to greater psychological flexibility and life satisfaction.
when_to_use: When feeling stuck, directionless, or like life is on autopilot; when trying to make a decision; when motivation is low and you need a reason bigger than immediate comfort.
when_not_to_use: During acute crisis or severe depression where basic stabilization is needed first; avoid using it to pressure someone into big life decisions in a single sitting, values work is ongoing, not a one-time fix.
step_by_step_exercise: Pick one life domain to reflect on (e.g., relationships, work, health, personal growth).
Ask yourself, 'If this area of my life were going really well, deep down, what would matter most to me here?'
Write down a few words or phrases that capture that (e.g., 'honesty', 'being present', 'curiosity').
Ask, 'What small action, this week, would move me a little closer to that value?'
Notice this is different from a goal: a value is a direction you can always walk toward, not a box to check off.
reflection_questions: Is the way you're currently spending your time and energy aligned with this value?
What gets in the way of acting on this value, and is it fear, habit, or something else?
common_mistakes: Confusing values (ongoing directions) with goals (achievable endpoints)
Choosing values based on what you 'should' want rather than what genuinely matters to you
Waiting to feel motivated before acting, instead of acting in the direction of the value regardless of mood
example_scenario: User: 'I don't even know what I want anymore, everything feels pointless.' Companion: 'That sounds heavy. Let's try something different than finding the answer, if your friendships were going really well, what would that actually look like? What would matter most to you there?'

title: Gentle Gratitude Reflection
summary: A brief, non-pressured practice of noticing small things that went okay or brought a flicker of positivity, without forcing positivity.
detailed_explanation: Gratitude practices have been linked in research, such as work by Emmons and McCullough, to improved mood, better sleep, and reduced stress over time, likely because they train attention toward positive or neutral details that negativity bias tends to filter out. The 'gentle' framing matters clinically: done without care, gratitude prompts can feel dismissive of real pain, so this version emphasizes noticing alongside difficulty, not instead of it.
when_to_use: As a regular, low-effort daily or weekly habit to build a more balanced view of life, especially during periods of stress where only the negative tends to stand out.
when_not_to_use: During acute grief, depression, or crisis, where being asked to find things to be grateful for can feel invalidating or add shame ('I should be grateful, what's wrong with me'); never present as a replacement for processing genuine pain.
step_by_step_exercise: Find a quiet moment, ideally at a consistent time like morning or bedtime.
Think back over the last day, or right now, in this moment.
Notice one to three small things, however minor, that were okay, pleasant, or helpful: a warm drink, a kind text, sunlight through a window.
Let yourself briefly linger on one of them and notice any sensation of ease it brings, even if small.
Optionally, write it down in a few words.
reflection_questions: Did any of these moments surprise you when you looked for them?
Is there a person, however small their role, you could thank today?
common_mistakes: Forcing positivity or pretending everything is fine when it isn't
Only counting 'big' things and dismissing small ones as not worth noticing
Using gratitude to bypass or suppress difficult feelings rather than holding both at once
example_scenario: User: 'Today was rough but I don't want to just dwell on the bad.' Companion: 'It's okay for today to have been genuinely hard. Alongside that, is there anything, even something tiny, that felt even a little bit good or easier today?'

title: Basic Cognitive Reframing
summary: A method for identifying an unhelpful automatic thought and consciously considering a more balanced, evidence-based alternative.
detailed_explanation: CBT theory holds that it's not situations themselves but our interpretation of them that drives emotional reactions. Reframing doesn't mean forcing positivity, it means slowing down to examine whether an automatic thought is accurate, helpful, and complete, then generating a more balanced perspective that accounts for evidence the original thought may have ignored. Over time this practice weakens automatic negative thinking patterns.
when_to_use: When a thought is causing distress and seems extreme, catastrophic, or overly certain (e.g., 'I always fail', 'Nobody likes me'); useful after a triggering event once initial intense emotion has settled slightly.
when_not_to_use: In the middle of acute panic or overwhelming emotion, when the analytical thinking required isn't accessible yet, ground or soothe first; do not use to dismiss or argue away genuinely valid concerns, since some negative thoughts are accurate and warrant action, not reframing.
step_by_step_exercise: Identify the specific automatic thought (e.g., 'I'm going to get fired').
Rate how strongly you believe it, 0-100%.
Ask, 'What evidence supports this thought? What evidence goes against it?'
Ask, 'Is there another way to look at this situation?'
Write a more balanced thought that still respects the evidence (e.g., 'I made a mistake, but one mistake doesn't mean I'll be fired, I can fix this').
Re-rate how strongly you believe the original thought now.
reflection_questions: What would you tell a friend who had this exact thought?
Is this thought a fact, or does it feel like a fact because the emotion is so strong?
common_mistakes: Jumping straight to 'positive thinking' instead of examining actual evidence
Trying to argue yourself out of a feeling rather than the thought driving it
Expecting one reframe to permanently erase a deeply ingrained thought pattern
example_scenario: User: 'My boss didn't say good morning to me, she must be planning to fire me.' Companion: 'That thought sounds really scary. Let's look at it together, what's the evidence for that, and is there any evidence against it, like maybe she was just distracted or in a hurry?'

title: Worry Time (Scheduled Worrying)
summary: Setting aside a specific, contained time each day to worry, so intrusive worries outside that window can be postponed rather than engaged with immediately.
detailed_explanation: This technique, drawn from CBT approaches to generalized anxiety, works by breaking the habit of worrying in response to every anxious thought throughout the day. Postponing worry, rather than suppressing it, which tends to backfire, reduces its overall grip because most postponed worries lose urgency or resolve themselves by the scheduled time, and it trains the brain that worry can be delayed without catastrophe occurring.
when_to_use: For chronic, repetitive worrying that intrudes throughout the day and interferes with concentration or tasks; useful for generalized anxiety patterns rather than a single acute concern.
when_not_to_use: For worries tied to an imminent, real danger requiring immediate action; not appropriate as a way to avoid worries indefinitely, the scheduled time must actually be used, not skipped.
step_by_step_exercise: Choose a consistent 15-20 minute 'worry time' each day, not too close to bedtime.
When a worry pops up outside that window, briefly jot it down in a few words on a 'worry list'.
Gently tell yourself, 'I'll think about this properly at worry time,' and redirect attention back to the present task.
During worry time, go through your list and allow yourself to fully think through each item.
At the end of the window, close the session deliberately, stand up, change activity, or take a breath to signal it's over.
reflection_questions: How many of the worries you postponed still felt urgent by the time worry time arrived?
What did you notice about your ability to refocus on tasks after postponing a worry?
common_mistakes: Using worry time as an excuse to worry all day 'because I'll deal with it later' without actually redirecting attention in between
Skipping worry time altogether, which can make postponed worries feel unresolved and build up
Scheduling it right before bed, which can interfere with sleep
example_scenario: User: 'I keep getting pulled into worrying about this trip next month, over and over, all day.' Companion: 'That sounds exhausting. What if we tried setting a specific 15-minute worry time later today, and for now, we jot the worry down in one line and gently come back to what you're doing?'

title: Short Body Scan
summary: A brief mindfulness practice of systematically directing attention through the body to notice sensations without trying to change them.
detailed_explanation: The body scan is a core mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR) exercise that builds interoceptive awareness, the ability to notice internal bodily signals, which is linked to better emotion regulation. By practicing non-judgmental observation of physical sensations, it helps break the habit of either ignoring the body's stress signals or catastrophizing them, and can reveal tension held unconsciously.
when_to_use: When feeling disconnected from your body, overwhelmed by thoughts, or as a grounding and relaxation practice at any time of day, including before sleep.
when_not_to_use: For individuals with significant body-focused trauma or somatic dissociation, where focusing on bodily sensations could be distressing without trauma-informed support; if sensations trigger panic, shift to external, non-body-focused grounding instead.
step_by_step_exercise: Sit or lie down comfortably and close your eyes or soften your gaze.
Take three slow breaths to settle in.
Bring attention to your feet, notice any sensation (warmth, tingling, pressure, or nothing at all) without judging it.
Slowly move attention upward: legs, hips, stomach, chest, hands, arms, shoulders, neck, and face.
At each area, pause for a few breaths, simply noticing what's there.
If your mind wanders, gently guide it back to the body without self-criticism.
Finish by taking a full breath and noticing your body as a whole.
reflection_questions: Which parts of your body were easy to feel, and which felt numb or hard to notice?
Did you find any areas holding tension you weren't previously aware of?
common_mistakes: Trying to force relaxation instead of simply observing sensations as they are
Rushing through the body instead of pausing at each area
Judging sensations as 'good' or 'bad' rather than just noticing them
example_scenario: User: 'I feel really disconnected from myself today, kind of numb.' Companion: 'Let's try a short body scan to gently reconnect. Start by bringing your attention down to your feet, what do you notice there, even if it's just the feeling of your socks or the floor?'

title: Small Behavioral Activation
summary: Deliberately scheduling one small, manageable activity to counter the withdrawal and inactivity that often deepens low mood.
detailed_explanation: Behavioral activation is grounded in the observation that depression often creates a downward spiral: low mood leads to withdrawal from activity, which removes sources of pleasure and accomplishment, which deepens low mood further. Rather than waiting to 'feel like it,' this technique reverses the spiral by starting with small, achievable action first, since behavior change often precedes and drives motivation and mood change, not the other way around.
when_to_use: When feeling low, unmotivated, stuck, or withdrawing from activities that used to matter; useful for building momentum out of a low-energy period.
when_not_to_use: During severe depression with significant impairment or safety concerns, where professional support is needed to determine appropriate pacing; avoid overloading with big tasks, the technique specifically requires starting small.
step_by_step_exercise: Think of one very small activity you could do in the next hour (e.g., open a window, make tea, send one text, step outside for 2 minutes).
Make sure it's genuinely small enough that it feels almost too easy to refuse.
Choose a specific time to do it.
Do the activity, focusing on simply completing it rather than enjoying it.
Afterward, briefly notice how you feel, physically and emotionally, without judging the result.
Consider adding one more small activity the next day, gradually building a list of what helps.
reflection_questions: What almost stopped you from doing this activity, and what helped you do it anyway?
Did your mood shift at all, even slightly, after completing it?
common_mistakes: Choosing an activity that's too big or ambitious for the current energy level
Waiting to feel motivated before acting, which can mean waiting indefinitely
Judging the activity as pointless if it doesn't produce dramatic mood improvement immediately
example_scenario: User: 'I haven't left my bed in two days, I just can't find the energy for anything.' Companion: 'That sounds really heavy. We don't need to fix everything at once, is there one tiny thing, like opening the curtains or drinking a glass of water, that might feel doable in the next few minutes?'

title: Simple Thought Defusion
summary: A technique for creating distance from a difficult thought by observing it as a mental event rather than an absolute truth.
detailed_explanation: In ACT, 'fusion' refers to becoming so entangled with a thought that it feels like objective reality rather than one mental event among many. Defusion techniques, like adding the phrase 'I'm having the thought that...' before a thought, create a small but meaningful gap between the thinker and the thought, reducing the thought's behavioral grip without needing to argue about whether it's true or false, a person can hold a thought lightly rather than being controlled by it.
when_to_use: When a thought feels sticky, repetitive, or overwhelming, especially self-critical or catastrophic thoughts that don't respond well to logical debate; useful alongside or instead of cognitive reframing.
when_not_to_use: When a thought reflects a genuine, actionable concern requiring real-world problem-solving rather than distancing; not a substitute for addressing thoughts related to safety or self-harm, which need direct attention and support.
step_by_step_exercise: Notice the difficult thought you're having (e.g., 'I'm a failure').
Silently or out loud, prepend the phrase: 'I'm having the thought that I'm a failure.'
Go a step further: 'I notice I'm having the thought that I'm a failure.'
Optionally, try saying the thought in a silly voice, singing it, or picturing it as text scrolling across a screen, to further highlight it as just a mental event.
Notice whether the thought feels slightly less 'true' or urgent after doing this.
reflection_questions: Did adding distance to the thought change how much power it had over you?
Is this thought one that shows up often? What tends to trigger it?
common_mistakes: Using defusion to try to make the thought disappear entirely, rather than simply changing your relationship to it
Treating defusion as a way to avoid ever examining whether a thought reflects something worth addressing
Doing it once and expecting the thought to never return, defusion is a skill to practice repeatedly
example_scenario: User: 'I keep thinking I'm going to ruin everything, and I can't shake it.' Companion: 'That thought sounds really loud right now. What if, just as an experiment, you tried saying it as: I'm noticing I'm having the thought that I'm going to ruin everything. See if that changes how it feels at all.'

title: Self-Soothing with the Five Senses
summary: A DBT distress tolerance skill that uses deliberately pleasant sensory input across sight, sound, smell, taste, and touch to comfort yourself in a difficult moment.
detailed_explanation: This is one of DBT's core distress tolerance skills, designed for moments when emotional pain is intense but the situation can't be changed right away. Deliberately engaging pleasant senses gives the nervous system something soothing to attend to, functioning as healthy self-care that doesn't require solving the underlying problem immediately, the goal is survival and comfort in the moment, not resolution.
when_to_use: During high emotional distress when the situation cannot be immediately fixed or changed, and you need to get through the moment without making things worse.
when_not_to_use: As a way to permanently avoid dealing with a problem that does need to be addressed eventually; if in crisis with safety concerns, prioritize direct safety measures and support over self-soothing alone.
step_by_step_exercise: Sight: Look at something pleasant, a photo you love, nature outside a window, a candle flame.
Sound: Play calming or comforting music, nature sounds, or sit in comforting silence.
Smell: Use a favorite scent, lotion, a candle, coffee, fresh air.
Taste: Slowly savor a comforting food or drink, like tea or a small piece of chocolate.
Touch: Wrap yourself in a soft blanket, pet an animal, or hold something with a comforting texture.
You don't need to do all five, even one or two can help; focus fully on the sensation for a few minutes.
reflection_questions: Which sense tends to be most soothing for you specifically?
What sensory items could you keep easily accessible for moments like this, a 'comfort kit'?
common_mistakes: Trying to think your way out of distress instead of engaging the senses directly
Rushing through the sensory experience instead of slowing down to actually notice it
Using this only as a last resort instead of proactively building a go-to sensory toolkit
example_scenario: User: 'I'm too upset to think straight, I just need to get through the next hour.' Companion: 'That makes sense. Let's focus on comfort, not solving anything right now. Do you have a blanket, music, or a scent nearby that usually feels soothing to you?'

title: Checking the Facts
summary: A DBT emotion regulation skill for examining whether the intensity of an emotional reaction actually fits the facts of the situation.
detailed_explanation: Emotions are often triggered by our interpretation of events rather than the events themselves, and DBT theory holds that when an emotion doesn't fit the actual facts, or is more intense than the situation warrants, acting on it is more likely to backfire. This skill walks through the situation, interpretations, and assumptions methodically to determine whether the emotional response matches reality, which helps decide whether to act on the emotion or use a different regulation strategy.
when_to_use: When an emotional reaction feels very intense, and you're unsure whether it's proportionate to the actual situation, before making an important decision or reacting.
when_not_to_use: As a way to dismiss or invalidate genuine, fact-based emotional reactions (e.g., grief after a real loss is fact-consistent and shouldn't be argued away); not useful in the middle of acute crisis when analysis isn't accessible.
step_by_step_exercise: Identify the emotion and rate its intensity 0-100%.
Identify the specific event that triggered it, just the facts, not your interpretation.
Ask, 'What am I assuming or interpreting about this situation?'
Ask, 'Are there other possible interpretations of the same facts?'
Ask, 'Does the intensity of my emotion actually fit the facts, or is it based more on assumptions, past experiences, or worst-case thinking?'
If the emotion doesn't fit the facts, consider using opposite action or another regulation skill.
reflection_questions: What assumption might you be adding on top of the actual facts?
How might someone with no emotional stake in this situation describe what happened?
common_mistakes: Confusing interpretations ('they're mad at me') with facts ('they didn't reply to my text')
Using this skill to suppress or dismiss valid emotions rather than genuinely check them
Doing this analysis while still highly activated, before any initial calming
example_scenario: User: 'My friend hasn't texted back in 3 hours, she must be furious with me.' Companion: 'Let's separate the facts from the story for a second. The fact is: no reply in 3 hours. The thought 'she's furious' is one possible interpretation, what are some other reasons that might explain it?'

title: Opposite Action
summary: A DBT skill of deliberately acting opposite to an emotion's urge when the emotion doesn't fit the facts or acting on it wouldn't be effective.
detailed_explanation: Every emotion comes with an action urge, fear urges avoidance, anger urges attack, sadness urges withdrawal. When an emotion is justified and helpful, acting on it makes sense, but when it's unjustified, disproportionate, or would make the situation worse, DBT teaches deliberately doing the opposite of the urge, which over repeated practice actually helps change the emotion itself, since behavior and emotion are closely linked.
when_to_use: When an emotion doesn't fit the facts of the situation (after checking the facts) or when acting on the urge would be ineffective or harmful, e.g., avoiding something out of unjustified fear, or isolating due to sadness that's fueling a depressive spiral.
when_not_to_use: When the emotion is justified and adaptive (e.g., fear in genuine danger, anger at real injustice), in these cases the action urge may be appropriate and useful; not meant to suppress emotions, only to change unhelpful action patterns.
step_by_step_exercise: Identify the emotion and its natural action urge (e.g., anxiety leads to avoidance; sadness leads to withdrawal).
Use 'Checking the Facts' to determine whether the emotion fits the situation.
If it doesn't fit, or acting on it wouldn't help, identify the opposite action (e.g., approach instead of avoid; engage instead of isolate).
Commit to doing the opposite action fully, with body language and tone matching, not half-heartedly.
Repeat as needed; opposite action often needs to be practiced multiple times before the emotion shifts.
reflection_questions: What is the action urge behind this emotion telling you to do?
What would doing the complete opposite, fully, look like in this moment?
common_mistakes: Doing the opposite action half-heartedly, which is much less effective than full commitment
Using opposite action on emotions that are actually justified and adaptive
Expecting one attempt to fully shift the emotion instead of practicing it consistently
example_scenario: User: 'I feel so anxious about this party I want to cancel and just stay home.' Companion: 'That urge to avoid makes sense with anxiety. Is the fear here really matching real danger, or could going anyway, even just for 20 minutes, be a form of opposite action that helps loosen anxiety's grip over time?'

title: Radical Acceptance
summary: A DBT distress tolerance skill of fully accepting reality as it is, without fighting it, in order to reduce the extra suffering caused by resistance.
detailed_explanation: DBT distinguishes pain, an inevitable part of life, from suffering, the additional distress created by refusing to accept painful reality. Radical acceptance means acknowledging reality completely, with the mind, body, and emotions, even when it's unwanted or unfair, not because it's okay, but because fighting reality that can't currently be changed adds a second layer of suffering on top of the original pain.
when_to_use: When facing a painful situation that cannot be changed right now (e.g., a loss, a diagnosis, a past event, someone else's choice), and resisting or denying it is adding to distress without changing the outcome.
when_not_to_use: For situations that can and should be changed, acceptance is not resignation or passivity about things within your control; not appropriate as a way to accept ongoing abuse, danger, or harm, which calls for safety action, not acceptance.
step_by_step_exercise: Notice you are fighting reality, look for thoughts like 'this shouldn't be happening' or 'this isn't fair' (which may be true, but resisting doesn't change the facts).
Remind yourself: 'This is what happened. Fighting it doesn't undo it.'
Acknowledge the reality with your whole self, say it plainly in your mind or out loud.
Practice accepting with your body too, unclench, breathe, let your posture soften, since physical resistance often mirrors mental resistance.
Notice that acceptance is not the same as approval or liking what happened, it's simply acknowledging that it is what it is, right now.
Allow this to be a practice you return to repeatedly, not a single decision.
reflection_questions: What is it costing you to keep fighting this reality?
What might become possible if you stopped resisting what already happened, even just a little?
common_mistakes: Confusing acceptance with approval, giving up, or saying the situation is fine
Trying to force acceptance all at once rather than allowing it to happen gradually
Using acceptance to avoid taking action on things that are actually within your control to change
example_scenario: User: 'I keep thinking about how things shouldn't have ended this way, I can't stop replaying it.' Companion: 'That kind of replaying makes sense, part of you is still fighting what happened. Radical acceptance isn't about being okay with it; it's about acknowledging, this is what happened, so the pain doesn't get doubled by also fighting reality itself. Would it help to explore what that might look like here?'

title: IMPROVE the Moment
summary: A DBT distress tolerance acronym offering a set of quick strategies to make a painful moment more bearable without making it worse.
detailed_explanation: IMPROVE is one of DBT's crisis survival skill sets, intended for moments of intense distress when the situation can't be resolved immediately. Each letter represents a distinct strategy, Imagery, Meaning, Prayer, Relaxation, One thing in the moment, Vacation, Encouragement, giving multiple options to try since different strategies work for different people and situations, the goal is tolerating the moment safely, not solving the underlying problem right away.
when_to_use: During intense emotional crisis or distress when urges to act destructively are high and the problem can't be fixed in this instant.
when_not_to_use: As a substitute for addressing a problem that does have a real solution available; if there's active risk of self-harm, this should be paired with direct safety planning and professional or crisis support, not used alone.
step_by_step_exercise: Imagery: Picture a safe, calming place or a successful way of coping with this situation.
Meaning: Try to find any purpose, lesson, or silver lining in the pain, without forcing it.
Prayer: If it fits your beliefs, turn to prayer or connection with something greater than yourself.
Relaxation: Try a body-based relaxation technique like slow breathing or muscle release.
One thing in the moment: Focus your full attention on just this one moment, one task at a time.
Vacation: Take a brief break, a few minutes away from the situation, like a short walk.
Encouragement: Cheerlead yourself with a supportive phrase, like 'I can get through this' or 'This feeling will pass.'
reflection_questions: Which of these strategies tends to work best for you personally?
Could you build a short list of your top 2-3 IMPROVE strategies to reach for quickly next time?
common_mistakes: Trying to use all seven strategies at once instead of picking one or two that fit the moment
Expecting these strategies to fix the underlying problem rather than simply making the moment survivable
Skipping straight to problem-solving instead of first stabilizing intense emotion
example_scenario: User: 'I'm so overwhelmed right now I don't know what to do with myself.' Companion: 'Let's try to get through this moment first. Would picturing a calming place help, or maybe stepping outside for a couple of minutes like a mini vacation? We can try a couple of quick things together.'

title: Building Mastery
summary: A DBT emotion regulation skill of intentionally doing an activity that creates a sense of accomplishment and competence, to build resilience against negative emotions.
detailed_explanation: Building Mastery is part of DBT's emotion-regulation skillset, aimed at reducing vulnerability to negative emotions by ensuring regular experiences of accomplishment. A steady diet of small, achievable challenges builds a reservoir of confidence and self-efficacy that acts as a buffer against future emotional difficulty, especially for people whose lives feel dominated by struggle or failure.
when_to_use: As a regular, ongoing practice, ideally daily or a few times a week, to build emotional resilience over time, especially useful during periods of low self-esteem or when life feels overwhelming and out of control.
when_not_to_use: During acute crisis when the priority is stabilization rather than building new skills; avoid choosing tasks so difficult they risk failure, which would undermine the goal rather than support it.
step_by_step_exercise: Pick an activity that is challenging but achievable, something slightly outside your comfort zone, not something guaranteed to fail or trivially easy.
Examples: learning a new recipe, doing a workout, organizing a drawer, finishing a small project, a puzzle.
Set aside dedicated time to work on it.
Complete the activity, even if imperfectly.
Afterward, deliberately acknowledge the accomplishment, notice, don't dismiss it ('I did that').
Gradually increase difficulty over time as confidence builds.
reflection_questions: What is one small skill or task you've been wanting to try but haven't yet?
How did it feel to notice and acknowledge your own accomplishment, rather than brushing past it?
common_mistakes: Choosing a task so difficult it's likely to end in failure, undermining the purpose
Completing the task but dismissing or minimizing the accomplishment instead of registering it
Treating this as a one-time fix instead of a repeated practice
example_scenario: User: 'I feel like I'm bad at everything lately, nothing I do feels like it matters.' Companion: 'That's a heavy feeling to carry. What if we picked one small, doable thing today, something a bit challenging but realistic, just to give yourself a concrete win to notice?'

title: Pleasant Events Scheduling
summary: Deliberately planning and scheduling enjoyable activities into your week to counteract the loss of pleasure common in low mood or depression.
detailed_explanation: This technique is rooted in behavioral activation theory: depression and chronic stress often reduce engagement in rewarding activities, which further lowers mood in a reinforcing cycle. By proactively scheduling pleasant events, rather than waiting to feel motivated, people re-expose themselves to sources of positive reinforcement, which over time can help lift mood and rebuild a sense of engagement with life.
when_to_use: When noticing a pattern of doing fewer enjoyable things, feeling flat or joyless, or when free time tends to be filled with avoidance (e.g., excessive scrolling) rather than genuine enjoyment.
when_not_to_use: During severe depression where even small activities feel impossible, start with Small Behavioral Activation first; avoid scheduling high-pressure or obligation-heavy 'fun' that could feel like another task.
step_by_step_exercise: Brainstorm a list of activities you've previously enjoyed or are curious to try, big or small (e.g., a walk, calling a friend, painting, a favorite show, cooking a new recipe).
Pick one or two for the coming week.
Schedule a specific day and time for them, treating it like an appointment with yourself.
When the time comes, follow through even if motivation is low, engagement often builds after starting, not before.
Afterward, rate how enjoyable or meaningful it actually was (0-10), noticing this may differ from your prediction beforehand.
reflection_questions: What did you predict the activity would feel like, and how did that compare to how it actually felt?
What activities used to bring you joy that you've drifted away from?
common_mistakes: Waiting until you 'feel motivated' to schedule anything, which can mean waiting indefinitely
Choosing overly ambitious or obligation-heavy activities that feel like chores
Skipping the follow-through when motivation dips, rather than treating it like a commitment
example_scenario: User: 'I used to love painting but I haven't touched my supplies in months.' Companion: 'That sounds like something worth reclaiming, even in a small way. What if you scheduled just 15 minutes this week, on a specific day, to sit down with your paints, no pressure to make anything good?'

title: Willing Hands and Half-Smiling
summary: A pair of DBT body-based acceptance skills that use open hands and a relaxed, slight smile to physically cue a stance of willingness rather than resistance toward a difficult moment.
detailed_explanation: DBT teaches that body posture and facial expression can influence emotional state, not just reflect it; clenched fists and a tense jaw tend to reinforce resistance and distress, while deliberately relaxing the hands into an open, upturned position and softening the face into a slight half-smile can cue the mind toward willingness and acceptance. These are physical companions to Radical Acceptance, making acceptance a felt, embodied practice rather than only a mental one.
when_to_use: When noticing physical resistance (clenched jaw, fists, tense shoulders) alongside emotional resistance to a painful reality; useful alongside or after Radical Acceptance work.
when_not_to_use: If the situation calls for active problem-solving or boundary-setting rather than acceptance (e.g., in an unsafe situation); half-smiling should never be used to mask genuine distress from others in a way that prevents seeking real support.
step_by_step_exercise: Notice areas of physical tension, especially your hands, jaw, and shoulders.
Unclench your hands and let them rest open, palms upward if seated, in your lap or at your sides.
Soften your facial muscles, especially around the eyes and mouth.
Let the corners of your mouth turn up very slightly, not a forced grin, just a gentle half-smile.
Pair this with a few slow breaths and, if helpful, a phrase like 'I am willing to accept this moment as it is.'
Hold this posture for a minute or two, noticing any shift in your internal stance toward the situation.
reflection_questions: Did changing your posture or expression shift anything about how you related to the difficult feeling?
Where in your body do you tend to hold resistance most often?
common_mistakes: Forcing a big smile rather than a subtle, relaxed one, which can feel fake or invalidating
Using half-smiling to suppress or hide genuine emotion instead of accompanying acceptance of it
Expecting the posture alone to eliminate the difficult emotion rather than support acceptance of it
example_scenario: User: 'I'm just gritting my teeth trying to get through today.' Companion: 'I hear how much you're bracing against it. Would you be willing to try something small, unclenching your hands, letting them rest open, and softening your face just slightly? Sometimes the body can lead the mind toward a bit more willingness.'

title: Core Principles of Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT)
summary: An overview of the foundational idea behind CBT, that thoughts, feelings, and behaviors are interconnected, and changing one can shift the others.
detailed_explanation: CBT, developed largely by Aaron Beck and Albert Ellis, is one of the most extensively researched forms of talk therapy, with strong evidence across depression, anxiety, and many other conditions. Its central premise is the cognitive model: situations don't directly cause emotions, our interpretation of them does, and by identifying and examining unhelpful thought patterns (cognitive distortions) alongside adjusting behaviors, people can change how they feel and function, often through structured, skills-based, present-focused work.
when_to_use: As a general framework for understanding why techniques like thought records, cognitive reframing, and behavioral activation work; useful context when someone wants to understand the reasoning behind their coping tools.
when_not_to_use: CBT concepts are not a substitute for a diagnosis or a licensed therapist's individualized treatment plan; the framework alone is not sufficient for complex trauma or conditions requiring specialized treatment approaches.
step_by_step_exercise: The Cognitive Triangle: thoughts, feelings, and behaviors continuously influence one another.
Automatic thoughts: rapid, often unexamined interpretations of events that shape emotional reactions.
Cognitive distortions: common unhelpful thinking patterns (e.g., all-or-nothing thinking, catastrophizing, mind-reading) that skew perception.
Behavioral experiments: testing beliefs through real-world action rather than assumption alone.
Present-focused and structured: CBT typically emphasizes current patterns and concrete, skills-based work over lengthy exploration of the past.
Collaborative and skills-based: the goal is to build tools the person can use independently, not create long-term reliance.
reflection_questions: Can you notice a recent moment where a thought, not just the situation itself, shaped how you felt?
Which cognitive distortion, if any, do you notice showing up most often for you?
common_mistakes: Assuming CBT means 'just think positive,' when it actually means examining thoughts for accuracy, not forcing positivity
Expecting insight into a thought pattern alone to be enough, without practicing behavioral change alongside it
Treating one CBT technique as a full replacement for therapy when facing complex or persistent difficulties
example_scenario: User: 'Why does naming my thoughts even help anything?' Companion: 'Great question. CBT is built on the idea that our thoughts, feelings, and actions are all connected, so when we notice and gently examine a thought, it can actually shift the emotion attached to it too. That's the theory behind most of the exercises we do together.'

title: Core Principles of Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT)
summary: An overview of ACT's core aim, building psychological flexibility by accepting difficult inner experiences while committing to value-driven action.
detailed_explanation: ACT, developed by Steven Hayes, departs from trying to eliminate or control difficult thoughts and feelings, arguing that the struggle against them, known as experiential avoidance, often causes more suffering than the thoughts and feelings themselves. Instead, ACT aims to build psychological flexibility, the ability to stay present, make room for discomfort, and still act in line with one's values, through six core processes often summarized as the 'Hexaflex.'
when_to_use: As background for understanding techniques like Values Reflection and Thought Defusion; useful for someone who has tried to 'get rid of' anxiety or sadness without success and needs a different relationship to those feelings.
when_not_to_use: ACT concepts alone are not a substitute for professional treatment of trauma, severe mental illness, or safety-critical situations; not intended to suggest people should simply 'accept' unsafe or harmful circumstances rather than take action to change them.
step_by_step_exercise: Acceptance: making room for difficult thoughts and feelings instead of fighting or avoiding them.
Cognitive defusion: relating to thoughts as mental events rather than absolute truths.
Being present: contacting the current moment with openness rather than being lost in past or future.
Self-as-context: recognizing a stable sense of self that observes experiences, distinct from the experiences themselves.
Values: clarifying what genuinely matters as an ongoing compass for action.
Committed action: taking concrete steps aligned with values, even alongside discomfort.
reflection_questions: Is there a feeling you've been trying hard to get rid of, and what has that struggle cost you?
What would it look like to take one small values-aligned action today, even if the difficult feeling stayed present?
common_mistakes: Confusing acceptance with liking or approving of a painful experience, rather than simply making room for it
Treating ACT as another way to eliminate negative feelings instead of changing one's relationship to them
Skipping values and committed action, and only practicing acceptance in isolation
example_scenario: User: 'I've tried everything to stop feeling anxious and nothing works.' Companion: 'It sounds exhausting to keep fighting it. ACT offers a different angle, instead of trying to eliminate anxiety, what if we focused on making a little room for it while still moving toward what matters to you? Would you be open to exploring that?'

title: Core Principles of Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT)
summary: An overview of DBT's central balance between acceptance and change, and its four core skill modules for managing intense emotions.
detailed_explanation: DBT was developed by Marsha Linehan, originally for chronic suicidality and borderline personality disorder, and is built on a 'dialectic,' holding two seemingly opposite truths at once: radically accepting yourself and your situation as they are right now, while also actively working to change and build a life worth living. This balance is delivered through four skill modules: mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, and interpersonal effectiveness.
when_to_use: As background context for DBT-based techniques like Radical Acceptance, Opposite Action, and IMPROVE the Moment; useful for anyone dealing with intense, fast-shifting emotions or urges to act impulsively under distress.
when_not_to_use: Full DBT is typically delivered as an intensive, structured program, often including individual therapy, group skills training, and phone coaching, these framework notes are educational context, not a replacement for that comprehensive treatment when it's clinically indicated.
step_by_step_exercise: Dialectics: holding acceptance and change together, rather than choosing one over the other.
Mindfulness: the foundational skill of observing and describing experience non-judgmentally in the present moment.
Distress tolerance: skills for surviving crises without making things worse (e.g., IMPROVE, self-soothing, radical acceptance).
Emotion regulation: skills for understanding and reducing vulnerability to intense, unwanted emotions (e.g., opposite action, checking the facts, building mastery).
Interpersonal effectiveness: skills for asking for what you need and setting boundaries while maintaining relationships and self-respect.
Behavioral focus: DBT emphasizes tracking patterns, such as via diary cards, and building concrete skills over time.
reflection_questions: Where in your life do you notice a tension between accepting things as they are and wanting to change them?
Which of the four DBT skill areas, mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, or interpersonal effectiveness, feels most relevant to what you're facing right now?
common_mistakes: Assuming acceptance means giving up on change, rather than holding both simultaneously
Trying to use distress tolerance skills as a long-term fix instead of pairing them with emotion regulation and life changes over time
Expecting DBT skills to eliminate intense emotions entirely instead of helping manage and survive them
example_scenario: User: 'I don't understand how I'm supposed to accept things and also try to change them, that seems contradictory.' Companion: 'That tension is actually the heart of DBT. It's not either/or, it's both: fully acknowledging where things are right now, while still working toward something different. Would it help to look at how that plays out with what you're dealing with?'

title: Core Principles of Self-Compassion (Neff's Three Components)
summary: An overview of Dr. Kristin Neff's model of self-compassion, built on three core components: self-kindness, common humanity, and mindfulness.
detailed_explanation: Neff's research-backed model defines self-compassion as treating yourself with the same warmth you'd offer a good friend during a hard time, rather than harsh self-criticism. It rests on three interacting components: self-kindness versus self-judgment, common humanity versus isolation, and mindfulness versus over-identification. Studies link higher self-compassion to lower anxiety and depression and greater resilience, and, contrary to a common fear, it is not linked to complacency, but rather to more, not less, motivation to grow, since it's not powered by fear of self-punishment.
when_to_use: As foundational context for self-compassion techniques like the Self-Compassion Break and Hand on Heart; useful for anyone whose inner voice tends toward harsh self-criticism.
when_not_to_use: For some trauma survivors, self-compassion practices can initially feel uncomfortable or even trigger grief or resistance, in these cases, a gradual, professionally supported introduction is more appropriate than diving straight in; not a replacement for addressing situations that require real boundaries or external change, not just an internal shift.
step_by_step_exercise: Self-kindness vs. self-judgment: actively offering yourself warmth and understanding in difficulty, rather than harsh criticism.
Common humanity vs. isolation: recognizing that imperfection and suffering are part of the shared human experience, not a personal, isolating flaw.
Mindfulness vs. over-identification: holding painful feelings in balanced awareness, rather than suppressing them or being completely swept away by them.
Fierce self-compassion: alongside tender self-compassion, Neff also describes a protective, boundary-setting, motivating form of self-compassion, such as saying no or standing up for yourself.
Self-compassion is distinct from self-esteem: it doesn't depend on comparing favorably to others or on success and failure.
reflection_questions: How does the way you talk to yourself after a mistake compare to how you'd talk to a close friend in the same situation?
Which of the three components, kindness, common humanity, or mindfulness, tends to be hardest for you to access?
common_mistakes: Confusing self-compassion with self-pity or making excuses, when it's actually linked to greater accountability and growth
Believing self-compassion will make you complacent or less motivated, when research shows the opposite
Practicing only one component, such as mindfulness, while neglecting the others, like common humanity
example_scenario: User: 'Isn't being hard on myself the only way I'll actually improve?' Companion: 'That's a really common belief, but research actually points the other way; self-compassion tends to build more sustainable motivation than self-criticism, because it isn't powered by fear. It's less about letting yourself off the hook and more about supporting yourself the way you'd support someone you care about.'
